one_line_takeaway: A single reusable, low-frequency curved-grid infrared patch, optimized to push visual features off a clean-category subspace/topology (not to spoof a specific label), transfers across CLIP backbones, generative VLM decoders (captioning/VQA), datasets, and real physical deployment -- exposing a representation-level, cross-task vulnerability in infrared vision-language systems that current image-repair and adversarial-training defenses only partially mitigate.
key_finding: On classification (Table I), UCGP reaches 94.33% ASR on OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 vs. the strongest baseline's 22.67% (QR Code), and still beats baselines on the most robust backbone tested, Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14 (34.33% vs. 20.67%), while retaining comparable naturalness scores to baselines. On captioning (Table II) UCGP causes the largest absolute score drops in all six model/encoder settings, ranging 15.24-19.52 points on a 0-100 GPT-5-judged consistency scale; on VQA (Table III) drops range 15.54-22.55 points on a 0-100 correctness scale -- consistently larger than HCB, AdvGrid, and QR Code. Physical deployment achieved average ASR (AASR) of 70.76% indoors and 73.96% outdoors across the tested distance/angle ranges. Cross-dataset transfer (Table IV, 5 datasets x 4 surrogate backbones) shows OpenAI CLIP as by far the most transferable surrogate (95.37-97.24% average ASR across all five source datasets) versus much weaker and more variable transfer for OpenCLIP (21.75-29.69%), Meta-CLIP (22.11-57.17%), and EVA-CLIP (21.35-64.58%). Cross-category extension (Table VI) shows Dog (98.67% ASR) generalizes even better than Person (94.33%), while more rigid/elongated categories Car (61.00%) and Bicycle (56.00%) are harder to attack with a fixed-size local patch. Ablations (Table VII, Fig. 9) show the curved-grid parameterization and the subspace loss are the dominant contributors: swapping to pixel-wise optimization drops ASR from 94.33% to 30.33%; straight-segment or random-topology grids drop it to near-failure (5-7%); removing the topology loss drops ASR to 68.33% and removing the subspace loss further to 35.00% (OpenAI CLIP). Defense evaluation (Table VIII) shows partial mitigation only: DW-1 (blind digital-watermark repair) reduces ASR 94.33%->45.33%; DW-2 (location-aware repair) to 24.67%; adversarial training against the original patch (AT-1) to 15.33% -- but an adaptively re-optimized patch against the hardened model (AT-2) rebounds ASR to 78.00%, showing static defenses do not close the underlying representation-level vulnerability.
core_contribution: The paper proposes UCGP (Universal Curved-Grid Patch), a universal physical adversarial patch framework specifically designed for infrared vision-language models (IR-VLMs). Unlike prior infrared physical attacks that target closed-set detectors/classifiers, UCGP is optimized to disrupt a shared visual-representation subspace (the 'clean-category manifold') rather than a specific label or prompt, so that a single reusable patch simultaneously degrades classification, image captioning, and VQA across different IR-VLM architectures. The patch is parameterized as a low-frequency curved Bezier grid (not raw pixels) to make it fabricable and physically deployable, and is optimized with MetaDE (a derivative-free evolutionary optimizer) under Expectation-over-Transformation (EOT) capture-condition sampling and thin-plate-spline (TPS) non-rigid cloth-deformation modeling.
threat_model: The attacker has query access to the IR-VLM's forward interface and can obtain classification/captioning/VQA outputs together with the normalized visual embedding z=f(x) from the image encoder, but has NO access to model gradients, parameters, or training data. The authors explicitly name this a 'representation-visible audit setting' -- weaker than white-box optimization but stronger than pure output-only black-box API access, since the continuous attack objective is built directly from exposed visual embeddings. The physical constraint is that one shared patch (attached to a person's clothing/target region) must be reused across images, viewpoints, distances, and poses without per-image re-optimization -- it cannot be regenerated per frame or per prompt.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion/Conclusion explicitly states: (1) the threat model assumes a 'representation-visible audit interface' where visual embeddings are exposed/logged/recoverable -- it does NOT address strict output-only/API-only black-box access, which is left as future work. (2) EOT/TPS physical modeling does not fully capture material-dependent thermal response, ambient temperature shifts, sensor nonlinearity, occlusion, or camera post-processing. (3) Attack strength is uneven and backbone/category-dependent -- e.g., Meta-CLIP is comparatively robust, and rigid/elongated categories (car, bicycle) show much lower ASR than person/dog, so a single aggregate ASR number is explicitly discouraged by the authors in favor of source-target transfer matrices. (4) No defense fully closes the vulnerability; adaptive re-optimization against a hardened model restores most of the attack's effectiveness, meaning the evaluated defenses are not robust guarantees. (5) The authors call for future work with jointly optimized placement, longer video capture, multiple thermal cameras, different patch materials, larger category sets with confidence intervals, and defenses combining image-level repair with representation-level robustness training.

datasets_used: Infrared-COCO (~17k infrared images synthesized from MS-COCO + ~12k matched QA pairs, from Infrared-LLaVA pipeline) -- main classification/captioning/VQA experiments
quantitative_results: Classification ASR on OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14: UCGP 94.33% vs. best baseline (QR Code) 22.67%
Classification ASR on Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14 (most robust backbone): UCGP 34.33% vs. best baseline (HCB) 20.67%
Classification ASR on OpenCLIP ViT-B/16: UCGP 61.67% vs. best baseline (HCB) 34.00%
Classification ASR on EVA-CLIP ViT-G/14: UCGP 48.00% vs. best baseline (AdvGrid) 27.67%
Captioning score drop (0-100 GPT-5-judged consistency, absolute points): UCGP 15.24-19.52 across 6 model/encoder settings vs. baselines' 4.04-13.74
VQA score drop (0-100 GPT-5-judged correctness, absolute points): UCGP 15.54-22.55 across 6 model/encoder settings vs. baselines' 4.20-15.82
Physical attack average ASR: 70.76% indoor (6.2-11.0m), 73.96% outdoor (7.8-12.6m)
Cross-dataset transfer average ASR by surrogate: OpenAI CLIP 95.37-97.24%, OpenCLIP 21.75-29.69%, Meta-CLIP 22.11-57.17%, EVA-CLIP 21.35-64.58%
Cross-category ASR (Infrared-COCO, N=300 each): Person 94.33%, Dog 98.67%, Car 61.00%, Bicycle 56.00%
Ablation (OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 classification ASR): Full UCGP 94.33% -> pixel-wise patch 30.33% -> straight-segment grid 7.00% -> random topology 5.33% -> standard DE (no MetaDE) 76.00%
Loss ablation (OpenAI CLIP): Full UCGP 94.33% -> w/o topology loss 68.33% -> w/o subspace loss 35.00%

Defense evaluation ASR (OpenAI CLIP, from 94.33% no-defense baseline): DW-1 (blind repair) 45.33% (-49.00pp), DW-2 (location-prior repair) 24.67% (-69.66pp), AT-1 (adversarial training, original patch) 15.33% (-79.00pp), AT-2 (adaptive re-optimized patch vs hardened model) 78.00% (-16.33pp, i.e. mostly recovers)
baselines_compared: HCB (Adversarial Infrared Blocks, block-based thermal patch, Hu et al. arXiv:2304.10712)
AdvGrid (multi-view black-box grid-prior infrared patch, Tiliwalidi et al., Applied Soft Computing 2025)
QR Code (Infrared Invisible Clothing pattern, Zhu et al., CVPR 2022)
